source: Bergeaud & Verluise (2023), PLOS ONE 18(12):e0295587. Definition = functional-application TASKS for Genome Editing (S2-1 annotation guidelines); keywords (S2.3.1) and CPC (S2.3.2) are the rule-based candidate criteria. Hard negatives follow the paper's 'augmented anti-seed' (rule-matched but human-rejected on the abstract).
definition: Genome Editing: technology whose invention actually performs one of the defining Genome Editing tasks (e.g. Target a specific DNA sequence; Break / cleave a DNA sequence (e.g. double-strand break); Edit a DNA sequence (insert / delete / replace)). A patent is SEED only if its title+abstract clearly shows it carrying out one or more of the in-scope tasks below. Matching a keyword or CPC class is NOT sufficient — it only makes the patent a candidate.

primary_decision_axis.name: performs_task_vs_rule_match_only
primary_decision_axis.rule: ACCEPT (SEED) only if the invention itself PERFORMS a Genome Editing in-scope task with a concrete mechanism. REJECT as a HARD negative if the patent matched the domain's keywords/CPC (so it looks relevant) but the abstract shows it does NOT perform the task — the domain term is background/prior-art/one-possible-application, or the real contribution is a different/adjacent mechanism. REJECT as an EASY negative if there is no domain signal at all (clearly another field).

in_scope_tasks.desc: Break / cleave a DNA sequence (e.g. double-strand break)
Edit a DNA sequence (insert / delete / replace)

key_technical_signals: clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat
out_of_scope_confusables: RULE-MATCH-BUT-NOT-THE-TASK: the abstract contains Genome Editing keywords (or the patent sits in a listed CPC class) but the invention does not actually perform an in-scope task — the paper's 'augmented anti-seed' / hard examples.
The Genome Editing term appears only as background, motivation, prior art, or one possible application, while the real contribution is a different mechanism.
A generic or adjacent method that shares Genome Editing vocabulary but pursues a different functional goal.
hard_negative_patterns: matched a Genome Editing keyword/CPC rule, but the abstract shows no in-scope task is performed -> hard negative
Genome Editing named only as an example/application; the invention is really about something else -> hard negative
shares terminology with the domain but the functional goal is different -> hard negative

score_anchors.0.9_1.0: the invention clearly PERFORMS a Genome Editing in-scope task with a concrete mechanism
score_anchors.0.6_0.75: an in-scope task is plausibly implied but the mechanism/scope is unclear, or signals are mixed -> boundary
score_anchors.0.25_0.4: matched the rules but the abstract shows it does NOT perform the task (rule-match look-alike) -> hard negative
score_anchors.0.0_0.2: no domain signal at all — clearly a different field -> easy negative
